figures.caption: Figure 2: Trustworthiness–length Pareto frontiers for the Qwen3-8B family. Each panel plots one trustworthiness metric against mean CoT token count. The black polyline marks the Pareto frontier.
Figure 1: Overview of the evaluation framework. Each compressed model is compared against its matched uncompressed baseline under a uniform protocol across three trustworthiness dimensions.
Table 2: Composite efficiency scores E (Eq. 1; α = 2, β = 0.5) and mean output length L (tokens) for all tabulated methods. E > 1 indicates net benefit; underlined entries mark E < 1 (net loss).

Figure 3: Trustworthiness–length Pareto frontiers across all model groups. Colors denote compression methods, marker size denotes model scale. The black polyline marks the Pareto frontier in each panel.

Table 4: Coverage policy for methods considered in this study. Our evaluation targets the currently available and reproducible subset of CoT compression methods rather than the full set of papers in the literature.

Table 1: Trustworthiness metrics across all evaluated model families. In the column headers, ↑ indicates that higher values are better and ↓ indicates that lower values are better. HarmBench reports refusal accuracy (%); AgentHarm reports harmful task completion rate (%), where a lower score indicates a safer model; MMLU-ProX reports multilingual accuracy (%); and FaithEval reports truthfulness (%). Gray rows denote uncompressed baselines. “—” indicates entries excluded under the benchmark scope described in Section 3.1.
